1.synthesis: In the right hand of the one seated on the throne is a scroll written on both sides, sealed with seven seals. A mighty angel asks who is worthy to open it — and no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth can open it. John weeps greatly, until one of the elders speaks: 'Weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll and its seven seals' (v.5). John turns and sees 'a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain, having seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth' (v.6). The Lamb takes the scroll from the right hand of the one on the throne.
1.historical_context: The sealed scroll has been interpreted as a will (requiring seven witnesses to seal under Roman law), a legal deed of purchase, or the scroll of history's unfolding — most likely it represents the divine plan for the consummation of history, sealed until the appointed time and Opener. The 'Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David' draws on Genesis 49:9–10 (Judah's scepter) and Isaiah 11:1–10 (the branch from Jesse's root, upon whom the Spirit rests) — Davidic messianic titles whose military and royal resonance is immediately inverted: the Lion John is told about becomes, when he turns to look, a slaughtered Lamb. The seven horns (complete power) and seven eyes (complete knowledge) of the Lamb correspond to the sevenfold Spirit he holds and distributes — the Lamb's omnipotence and omniscience are expressed in the Spirit's fullness. The scroll 'written on both sides' echoes the tablets of the law (Exod 32:15) — the decree is complete and full, nothing more can be added to God's purposes.
1.literary_structure: The central interpretive move of the entire Revelation occurs at the hinge of vv.5–6: John is told 'the Lion … has conquered' and turns to see 'a Lamb … as though it had been slain.' The method of conquest is the cross. Every subsequent vision of the book must be read through this lens: the seals, trumpets, and bowls disclose the purposes of the slaughtered Lamb, not of a military conqueror in the conventional sense. The Lamb 'standing' despite having been slain captures the resurrection paradox: death is visible (slain) but not final (standing). The Lamb's position 'between the throne and the four living creatures and among the elders' (v.6) places him at the center of the heavenly court — the point where creation-worship (living creatures) and human-representative worship (elders) converge on the Redeemer.
1.theological_application: Gill on the Lion/Lamb paradox: 'Though he is a Lion, he is a Lamb; he is meek and lowly, harmless and inoffensive, pure and holy, and though a Lamb, yet not a weak one, but one that has seven horns, denoting his great power and strength, and with which he pushes his enemies, and none can stand before him.' Barnes on the sealed scroll: 'The whole symbolical representation seems designed to refer to the purpose of God in regard to future events; not a purpose formed at that moment, but one formed of old.' The scene of John's weeping (v.4) captures the pastoral pathos beneath the visionary grandeur: without an authorized opener, the future remains opaque, the martyrs' deaths seem unanswered, the tyrants' power seems unchecked. The elder's 'weep no more' (v.5) is the word that changes the frame: the future is not opaque — it has been opened by one who conquered through the cross. The sealed book of divine purpose has an opener, and history is not out of control.
1.christology: The Lion/Lamb identification is Revelation's most compressed christological statement. The expectation (Lion, David's royal heir, military conqueror) gives way to the reality (Lamb, slaughtered, yet standing), revealing that the method of messianic conquest was unexpected: not military victory over Rome but sacrificial death that becomes the ground for all subsequent divine purposes. The seven horns and seven eyes of the Lamb — complete power and complete knowledge — identify the slaughtered Lamb not as a victim but as the omnipotent omniscient one who chose the cross. The taking of the scroll from the Father's right hand (v.7) images the authority-transfer that Christ speaks of in Matthew 28:18: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.' The Lamb receives the Father's sealed purposes and alone is qualified to disclose and execute them.

8.synthesis: When the Lamb takes the scroll, the four living creatures and twenty-four elders fall before him with harps and golden bowls of incense ('which are the prayers of the saints'), and they sing a new song: 'Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation, and you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth' (vv.9–10). Then an innumerable host of angels joins: 'Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing!' (v.12). Finally every creature in heaven, earth, sea, and under the earth joins the chorus, blessing both 'him who sits on the throne and … the Lamb' (v.13).
8.historical_context: The 'new song' (v.9) has OT precedent as the song sung at a decisive new act of divine redemption: Psalm 96:1 ('sing to the Lord a new song'), Psalm 98:1, and Isaiah 42:10 all call for a new song in response to a new exodus or victory. The new song here celebrates a new Exodus — ransoming people 'from every tribe and language and people and nation' (v.9) echoes Exodus language (the word 'ransomed' is ēgorasas, purchased/redeemed) and the four-fold formula for total humanity appears repeatedly in Revelation as the scope of the Lamb's purchase. The sevenfold doxology of v.12 — power, wealth, wisdom, might, honor, glory, blessing — mirrors the highest form of praise in Jewish tradition; the same sevenfold pattern appears in 1 Chronicles 29:11 for God alone. The incense as prayers of the saints (v.8) integrates the worshipping community on earth into the heavenly liturgy: the suffering churches' prayers ascend before the throne and are part of the unfolding of the scroll's purposes.
8.literary_structure: The worship in ch.5 expands concentrically outward: the four living creatures and elders (vv.8–10) → myriads of angels (vv.11–12) → every creature in all creation (vv.13–14). Each circle adds voices; the object of the final, all-creature doxology is both the Father and the Lamb together — a bipartite worship that the book will sustain throughout. The 'new song' (v.9) is explicitly new: not because novelty is valued but because the reality being celebrated (redemption through the Lamb's blood from every nation) had never before occurred. The phrase 'by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation' (v.9) is the theological center of the chapter — the ground for the Lamb's worthiness is not his power or wisdom but his blood, and the scope of that blood's reach is universal.
8.theological_application: Gill on 'the prayers of the saints' as incense (v.8): 'The prayers of the saints, which are compared to incense for their efficacy, being put up in the name of Christ, and through his mediation and intercession, being free and voluntary, like incense which ascends upwards of itself, and for their acceptableness to God, being sweet odours to him.' Barnes on the new song's ground: 'The word "slain" here refers to the violent death of the Redeemer, and the new song is based on this — that he died for man, and thus became entitled to receive glory. The peculiarity of this song is that it is "new" — that is, it celebrates a work which had never before been celebrated.' The kingdom-and-priests formula (v.10: 'you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God') directly quotes 1:6 and Exodus 19:6: the worshipping community in every nation constitutes the covenant people that Sinai anticipated, now gathered through the Lamb's blood across all ethnic and linguistic boundaries.
8.christology: The co-worship of the Father and the Lamb in v.13 ('to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever') is one of the clearest expressions in Revelation of the Lamb's divine status: the same eternal doxology is directed to both. The 'Amen' of the four living creatures (v.14) and the prostration of the elders seal it: what creation has said of the Creator (ch.4) it now says equally of the Redeemer. The worthiness formula differs: God receives worship as Creator ('you created all things,' 4:11); the Lamb receives worship as Redeemer ('you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people,' 5:9). Together they establish that creation and redemption — the two great works of God — have a single ultimate object of worship: the Father on the throne and the Lamb who stands before it.
